skill.name: git-workflow-enforcer
skill.description: Helps enforce Git workflow conventions for commits, branches, pull requests, hooks, changelogs, and repository contribution practices.
skill.summary: Guides consistent Git commits, branch names, pull requests, hooks, and changelog workflows.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and weak-crypto matches, but most are markdown examples for Git workflow guidance or false positives from git terminology. Confirmed concerns are legitimate but elevated: the skill allows Bash and Write, suggests npm/npx execution, creates hook/template files, and shows a global Git configuration change under the home directory.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill requests Bash access and provides shell examples for npm scripts, git log filtering, npx standard-version, and Git configuration. This is expected for a Git workflow skill, but running npm or npx in an untrusted repository can execute project-controlled or downloaded code, so command execution should remain user-reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are real shell commands and the skill declares Bash access. The intent appears legitimate, but npm and npx execution are meaningful risks in untrusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Global Git Configuration and Home Directory Template
medium_findings.description: The skill shows creating a .gitmessage file and setting it as the global commit template. This is not malicious, but it changes user-level Git behavior outside the current repository and writes to a hidden file in the home directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The home-directory path and global Git configuration command are directly present. The behavior is documented and workflow-related, which lowers concern from high to medium.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick, shell substitution, and weak cryptographic algorithm matches come from markdown code fences, inline Git examples, conventional commit text, or git hash terminology. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution, cryptographic implementation, malware, network exfiltration, or prompt injection in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation, templates, or Git examples rather than executable Ruby or weak cryptographic code. Manual review found no hidden override instructions or exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: Secret Keyword Scan Is Defensive
low_findings.description: The api_key, password, and secret terms appear in a pre-commit hook example that blocks staged sensitive data. This is a defensive scan pattern rather than an attempt to read or transmit credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command searches staged diffs and exits with an error when secret-like text is found. There is no network sink or storage destination for matched data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Workflow Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell snippets for npm, git, grep, npx, and git config operations. These are useful for workflow enforcement but can affect the local repository or execute package scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are visible and semantically command-execution guidance. The surrounding context is legitimate Git workflow documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes for Templates and Hooks
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can create pull request templates, commit message hooks, and commit templates. These writes are expected but should be reviewed before applying them to a repository or global Git configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and template content are explicitly documented. The risk depends on whether the user asks the assistant to write these files.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose consistency across commit messages, branch names, pull requests, and release notes. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply repeatable Git workflow checks and templates.
actual_capabilities: Reviews repository conventions before suggesting workflow changes.
Formats commit messages using Conventional Commits patterns.
Suggests branch names with type prefixes and descriptive slugs.
Drafts pull request templates with testing and review checklists.
Provides sample pre-commit and commit-msg hooks for local validation.
Groups conventional commits into changelog-oriented categories.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples; enforcement depends on user-approved file changes or commands.
It does not verify remote branch protection settings through a hosting provider API.
Shell commands and npm or npx examples require review before use in untrusted repositories.
Global Git configuration examples can affect repositories outside the current project.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Commits
use_cases.description: Define clear commit message rules so team history is easier to review, search, and release.

use_cases.title: Prepare Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Create branch names, commit messages, and pull request checklists that match project conventions.

use_cases.title: Improve Release Notes
use_cases.description: Use conventional commit categories to organize changelog entries before tagging a release.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this commit message for Conventional Commits format and suggest a corrected version if needed: <message>

prompt_templates.title: Create a Branch Name
prompt_templates.prompt: Suggest a branch name for this task using the repository workflow conventions: <task description>

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Pull Request Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request template for this repository. Include description, type of change, testing, checklist, and related issues sections.

prompt_templates.title: Audit the Git Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the current repository conventions and recommend commit, branch, hook, and pull request workflow improvements. Ask before changing files or running commands.

output_examples.input: I fixed the login bug and need a commit message.
output_examples.output: Suggested commit message: fix(auth): handle login error state. Include a short body if the change affects users or closes an issue.

output_examples.input: Create a branch name for adding dark mode settings.
output_examples.output: Suggested branch name: feature/add-dark-mode-settings. Use an issue number prefix if your team requires one.

output_examples.input: Review our PR process for missing checks.
output: Add a testing section to the pull request template.
Document related issues in every pull request.

best_practices: Review generated hooks and templates before writing them into a repository.
Prefer repository-local configuration unless a global Git setting is explicitly required.
Run npm, npx, and git commands only after checking the repository trust level.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated shell commands automatically in an untrusted repository.
Do not apply global Git configuration when only one project needs the behavior.
Do not force a convention that conflicts with existing documented project standards.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: No. It helps format and validate commit messages. The user still controls commits and command execution.

faq.question: Can it detect existing repository conventions?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs the assistant to check contribution files, templates, commit patterns, and branch naming patterns.

faq.question: Does it support Conventional Commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides types, formats, examples, and validation guidance for Conventional Commits.

faq.question: Can it create pull request templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a pull request template structure with description, change type, testing, checklist, and related issues.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses Bash and Write guidance for Git workflow tasks. Some examples can execute local scripts or change global Git settings.

faq.question: Should I use it in untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review all shell commands, hooks, and generated files before approving changes or execution.
